paper_title: MoCapAnything V2: End-to-End Motion Capture for Arbitrary Skeletons

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent methods for arbitrary-skeleton motion capture from monocular video follow a factorized pipeline, where a Video-to-Pose network predicts joint positions and an analytical inverse-kinematics (IK) stage recovers joint rotations. While effective, this design is inherently limited, since joint positions do not fully determine rotations and leave degrees of freedom such as bone-axis twist ambiguous, and the non-differentiable IK stage prevents the system from adapting to noisy predictions or optimizing for the final animation objective. In this work, we present the first fully end-to-end framework in which both Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation are learnable and jointly optimized. We observe that the ambiguity in pose-to-rotation mapping arises from missing coordinate system information: the same joint positions can correspond to different rotations under different rest poses and local axis conventions. To resolve this, we introduce a reference pose-rotation pair from the target asset, which, together with the rest pose, not only anchors the mapping but also defines the underlying rotation coordinate system. This formulation turns rotation prediction into a well-constrained conditional problem and enables effective learning. In addition, our model predicts joint positions directly from video without relying on mesh intermediates, improving both robustness and efficiency. Both stages share a skeleton-aware Global-Local Graph-guided Multi-Head Attention (GL-GMHA) module for joint-level local reasoning and global coordination. Experiments on Truebones Zoo and Objaverse show that our method reduces rotation error from ~17 degrees to ~10 degrees, and to 6.54 degrees on unseen skeletons, while achieving ~20x faster inference than mesh-based pipelines. Project page: https://animotionlab.github.io/MoCapAnythingV2/

sections.title: Start
sections.content: MoCapAnything V2: End-to-End Motion Capture for Arbitrary Skeletons KEHONG GONG, Huawei Technologies Co., Ltd, Singapore ZHENGYU WEN, Central Research Institute, Singapore DAO THIEN PHONG, Central Research Institute, Singapore MINGXI XU, Central Research Institute, Singapore WEIXIA HE, Central Research Institute, Singapore QI WANG, Central Research Institute, Singapore NING ZHANG, Central Research Institute, Singapore ZHENGYU LI, Central Research Institute, Singapore GUANLI HOU, Central Research Institute, Singapore DONGZE LIAN, Central Research Institute, Singapore XIAOYU HE, Central Research Institute, Singapore MINGYUAN ZHANG, Central Research Institute, Singapore HANWANG ZHANG, Central Research Institute, Singapore Fig. 1. Overview of MoCapAnything V2. Given an input video of human or an animal, our method infers topology-agnostic skeleton sequence across diverse skeleton topologies. Conditioned on reference asset, the model predicts animation-ready rotations via an end-to-end framework, enabling the reference asset to perform the input motion. Recent methods for arbitrary-skeleton motion capture from monocular video [Gong et al. 2025] follow factorized pipeline, where Video-toPose network predicts joint positions and an analytical inverse-kinematics (IK) stage recovers joint rotations. While effective, this design is inherently limited, since joint positions do not fully determine rotations and leave degrees of freedom such as bone-axis twist ambiguous, and meanwhile the Equal contribution. In this work, we instead present the first fully end-to-end framework, where both Video-toPose and Pose-to-Rotation are learnable and jointly optimized. Crucially, we observe that the ambiguity in pose-to-rotation mapping arises from missing coordinate-system information, as the same joint positions can correspond to different rotations under different rest poses and local axis conventions, and to resolve this we introduce reference poserotation pair from the target asset, which together with the rest pose not only anchors the mapping but also 6 2 0 2 0 3 ] . This turns rotation prediction into well-constrained conditional problem and enables effective learning. In addition, our model predicts joint positions directly from video without relying on mesh intermediates, which improves both robustness and efficiency, and both stages share skeleton-aware Global-Local Graph-guided Multi-Head Attention (GL-GMHA) module for joint-level local reasoning and global coordination. Experiments on Truebones Zoo and Objaverse show that our method reduces rotation error from 17 to 10, and to 6.54 on unseen skeletons, while achieving 20 faster inference than mesh-based pipelines. Additional Key Words and Phrases: Motion capture, arbitrary skeleton, monocular video, end-to-end learning, inverse kinematics, character animation, cross-skeleton generalization

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Recovering 3D character animation from monocular video, commonly referred to as motion capture, is long-standing problem at the intersection of computer vision and computer graphics. While recent deep learning approaches have achieved strong performance for human motion capture [Goel et al. 2023; Kanazawa et al. 2018a; Kocabas et al. 2020; Pavllo et al. 2019], extending this capability to the arbitrary-skeleton setting remains fundamentally challenging. In this setting, the goal is to recover motion directly in the parameter space of target rigged asset given only monocular video and reference skeleton [Gong et al. 2025]. The difficulty arises from the large variation in skeleton topology, joint count, and especially local coordinate conventions across different assets. central challenge lies in recovering joint rotations. Unlike joint positions, rotations are defined with respect to skeleton-specific coordinate system determined by the rest pose and local axis conventions. As result, the same joint positions can correspond to different rotations under different skeletons, making direct videoto-rotation prediction prone to overfitting and poor generalization. To mitigate this issue, existing methods adopt factorized design. learned Video-to-Pose (VP) network first predicts 3D joint positions, and an analytical inverse-kinematics (IK) solver then converts these positions into joint rotations. This design leverages the fact that joint positions are largely shared across skeletons performing the same motion, thereby improving generalization at the pose level. However, this factorization introduces fundamental limitation. Since joint positions do not fully determine rotations, the pose-to-rotation mapping is inherently under-constrained, and analytical solvers cannot resolve degrees of freedom such as boneaxis twist, nor can they adapt to the noise distribution of predicted poses. Moreover, the non-differentiable IK stage prevents the two components from being jointly optimized, forcing the pose predictor to ignore the downstream rotation objective. In this work, we present the first fully end-to-end framework for arbitrary-skeleton motion capture from monocular video, in which both the Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation (PR) stages are learnable and jointly optimized. The key challenge lies in the ill-posed nature of the PR mapping: the same 3D joint positions can correspond to different rotation values under different rest poses and local coordinate frames, and therefore pose alone is insufficient to determine rotations, as it does not specify how the target skeleton defines its rotation coordinate system. To address this issue, we extend the reference signal. While prior work relies on static geometry (skeleton, mesh, and rendered views) [Gong et al. 2025], we additionally introduce single reference poserotation pair from the target asset, which is naturally available for any rigged skeleton. Together with the rest pose, this reference acts as an explicit coordinate-system anchor, turning the ill-posed PR mapping into well-constrained conditional prediction problem that can be modeled by neural decoder. This design enables the PR stage to be learned, and more importantly, unlocks end-to-end training of the entire pipeline. Gradients from the rotation objective can now flow back through the pose intermediate and into the visual encoder, so that the pose representation is no longer optimized solely for positional accuracy, but is adaptively reshaped into representation that better supports rotation recovery. In addition, we remove the mesh intermediate used in prior work [Gong et al. 2025]. While mesh representations can be beneficial when accurate, predicted mesh sequences often introduce noise and lead to error accumulation. By directly predicting joint positions from video, our approach improves robustness and significantly reduces computational cost, achieving approximately 20 faster inference. To support both stages, we introduce skeleton-aware attention mechanism, Global-Local Graph-guided Multi-Head Attention (GL-GMHA), which alternates between local kinematic-chain reasoning and global cross-branch coordination. This design enables effective modeling of both structural constraints and global motion dependencies across diverse skeletons. We evaluate our method on the Truebones Zoo dataset, which covers seen, rare, and unseen skeletons, as well as the Objaverse benchmark. Our approach significantly improves rotation accuracy across all settings, especially on unseen skeletons, while also providing substantial gains in efficiency. Our main contributions are as follows: End-to-end learnable Video-to-Pose-to-Rotation framework. We present the first fully end-to-end trainable pipeline for arbitrary-skeleton motion capture, enabling joint optimization of pose and rotation. Reference-conditioned rotation modeling. We introduce reference poserotation pair that, together with the rest pose, defines the rotation coordinate system and resolves the ambiguity of pose-to-rotation mapping. Efficient direct video-to-pose prediction. We eliminate the mesh intermediate, improving robustness and achieving approximately 20 faster inference. Skeleton-aware attention mechanism. We propose GLGMHA, which integrates local kinematic reasoning with global skeleton coordination in unified attention framework.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Recovering animation-ready motion from monocular video requires bridging the gap between observable visual cues (e.g., joint positions) and skeleton-specific motion parameters (e.g., rotations). Existing approaches address different aspects of this problem, but none fully resolve the ambiguity of mapping pose to rotation under varying skeleton definitions. We review three related directions.

sections.title: 2.1 Pose Estimation
sections.content: Pose estimation methods aim to localize anatomical keypoints from images or videos. Classic approaches include heatmap-based architectures such as HRNet [Sun et al. 2019] and regression-based methods, while recent transformer-based frameworks such as ViTPose [Xu et al. 2022b] and DETR-style models [Carion et al. 2020; Shi et al. 2022; Xiao et al. 2022] perform end-to-end keypoint prediction without hand-crafted grouping. To improve generalization beyond fixed categories, category-agnostic pose estimation (CAPE) methods [Hirschorn and Avidan 2023; Rusanovsky et al. 2024; Shi et al. 2023; Xu et al. 2022a] (e.g., POMNet, CapeFormer, Pose Anything, CapeX) predict keypoints for unseen objects by matching support and query representations. These approaches significantly improve cross-category generalization and are directly relevant to our setting, which also involves diverse skeleton structures. However, all these methods focus on keypoint localization and operate either in 2D or in fixed keypoint space. They do not model 3D motion trajectories, temporal dynamics, or skeleton-specific parameterizations, and thus cannot directly produce animationready joint rotations. In particular, they do not address how the same joint configuration should be interpreted under different skeleton coordinate systems.

sections.title: 2.2 Motion Capture
sections.content: Monocular motion capture has been extensively studied in the context of parametric human models such as SMPL [Loper et al. 2015] and SMPL-X [Pavlakos et al. 2019]. These methods regress pose and shape parameters directly from images or videos, using architectures ranging from optimization-based fitting (e.g., SMPLifyX) to feed-forward networks such as VIBE [Kocabas et al. 2020] and recent transformer-based models. While effective, these approaches operate in fixed parameter space and are tightly coupled to predefined skeletons, limiting their applicability to arbitrary animatable assets. Beyond humans, model-free reconstruction methods (e.g., CMR [Kanazawa et al. 2018b], LASSIE [Yao et al. 2022], MagicPony [Wu et al. 2023], 3D-Fauna [Li et al. 2024]) recover deformable surfaces without relying on explicit templates, and videobased extensions (e.g., BANMo [Yang et al. 2022], LASR [Yang et al. 2021]) model temporal consistency. Model-based approaches such as SMAL [Zuffi et al. 2017] extend parametric modeling to specific animal categories. However, these methods either lack explicit skeletal parameterization or remain tied to species-specific templates, and therefore do not generalize to arbitrary skeletons required for animation. Recent work on arbitrary-skeleton motion capture addresses this limitation by conditioning on reference asset. MoCapAnything [Gong et al. 2025] adopts factorized design, where learned Video-to-Pose network predicts joint positions and an analytical inverse kinematics (IK) solver recovers joint rotations. This decomposition improves cross-skeleton generalization by leveraging pose as shared intermediate representation. MoCapAnything V2: End-to-End Motion Capture for Arbitrary Skeletons Nevertheless, this design introduces fundamental limitation. The mapping from joint positions to rotations is inherently underconstrained: the same pose can correspond to different rotations under different rest poses and local coordinate systems. Analytical IK solvers resolve this ambiguity using hand-crafted constraints, but cannot capture motion priors, resolve twist ambiguity, or adapt to noisy pose predictions. Moreover, the non-differentiable IK stage prevents end-to-end optimization, limiting the ability of the system to align intermediate pose representations with the final rotation objective. In contrast, we explicitly address the ambiguity of poseto-rotation mapping by introducing reference poserotation pair that defines the underlying coordinate system. This enables us to cast pose-to-rotation as learnable conditional problem and train the entire Video-to-Pose-to-Rotation pipeline end-to-end.

sections.title:
3 Method
3.1 Problem Formulation
Given an input video V = {𝐼1, . . . , 𝐼𝑇 } and a target skeleton S, our
goal is to recover a sequence of 3D joint motion parameters that are
consistent with the observed video and expressed in the parameter
space of S. The target skeleton is defined by a joint hierarchy (parent
indices 𝝅), bone offsets o ∈ R𝐽 ×3 (the rest pose), and per-joint
semantic labels. We assume S is a tree-structured rigged skeleton
with a single root; the number of joints 𝐽 and the topology are
otherwise arbitrary. In practice, we set the maximum joint count
to 150 to accommodate the largest skeleton in our training data
(143 joints); this bound is purely a padding/memory choice and can
be adjusted for other datasets. We seek to predict per-frame joint
rotations R = {r1, . . . , r𝑇 }, where each r𝑡 ∈ R𝐽 ×6 is parameterized as
a 6D rotation representation [Zhou et al. 2019], such that applying
forward kinematics (FK) with R on S reproduces the motion seen
in the video.
sections.content: To improve modeling stability and cross-skeleton generalization, we decompose the problem into two stages mediated by an explicit intermediate representation: Video Stage 1 Pose Stage 2 Rotation, (1) where pose = {p1, . . . , p𝑇 }, p𝑡 R𝐽 3, denotes the 3D joint positions in canonical coordinate frame, and rotation denotes the local joint rotations in the target skeletons coordinate system.

sections.title: 3.2 Overview
sections.content: Unlike prior arbitrary-skeleton methods [Gong et al. 2025], which adopt factorized design, learned Video-to-Pose stage followed by non-differentiable analytical IK stage for Pose-to-Rotation (See Fig. 2), our framework casts both stages as learnable neural modules and trains them jointly end-to-end (see Fig. 3). This unified design is what makes joint optimization of the full VPR pipeline possible; its technical enabler is reference poserotation pair that resolves the ill-posedness of learnable PR mapping (3.5). Concretely, our framework comprises two core modules: Video-to-Pose module (3.4) predicts sequence of joint positions from the input video, conditioned on reference frame that establishes the skeleton-specific joint layout. Gong and Wen, et al. Fig. 2. Comparison of MoCapAnything V1 and V2. Unlike V1, which depends on mesh-conditioned video-to-pose estimation and analytical inverse kinematics (IK) for rotation recovery, V2 eliminates mesh conditioning and introduces fully learnable Pose2Rot module. The entire pipeline is optimized end-to-end, enabling bidirectional coupling between pose and rotation for improved robustness and animation-ready motion synthesis. Pose-to-Rotation module (3.5) maps the predicted joint positions to per-joint rotations, conditioned on the target skeletons rest pose and reference poserotation pair that anchors the local coordinate system. Both modules are conditioned on the same reference frame, providing consistent anchor throughout the pipeline: during training, this frame is randomly sampled from the ground-truth sequence, while at test time it is naturally provided together with the target skeleton asset (e.g., one frame from the rigged animation supplied with the asset).

sections.title: 3.3 Global-Local Graph-Guided Multi-Head Attention
sections.content: We adopt topology-aware attention mechanism to encode skeletal structure. Building upon Graph-guided Multi-Head Attention (GMHA) [Gat et al. 2025], we incorporate graph-derived joint relations, including distance and kinematic connectivity, to guide attention across arbitrary skeletons. To better capture articulated motion, we extend GMHA into Global-Local variant (GL-GMHA) by alternating between two complementary attention patterns. Local layers restrict attention along kinematic chains to model intra-limb dependencies, while global layers allow full connectivity to capture cross-limb coordination and whole-body dynamics. We employ GL-GMHA as shared structural backbone across both the Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation modules, ensuring consistent topology-aware reasoning throughout the pipeline. This design enables joint modeling of local constraints and global coherence without additional parameters, and naturally generalizes to diverse skeleton topologies.

sections.title: 3.4 Video-to-Pose Module
sections.content: This module predicts joint positions from the input video, without relying on explicit geometric intermediates such as mesh or surface normals. While our experiments (4.3) confirm that ground-truth mesh can improve pose estimation when available, predicted mesh introduces error propagation that degrades overall stability. We therefore adopt purely vision-driven design. Reference query encoder. To ground the joint layout in the target skeleton, we first encode reference frame, defined as single frame for which the joint positions and corresponding image features are both known. The reference joint positions pref R𝐽 3 are mapped through frequency-based positional embedding [Mildenhall et al. 2020] and projected to query dimension 𝑑. Per-joint semantic embeddings, obtained by encoding joint names with the T5 [Raffel et al. 2020] text encoder, are added to provide category-agnostic joint identity; these embeddings serve as semantic positional identifiers and enable the model to generalize across skeletons with arbitrary joint counts and naming conventions. The reference image features zref R𝑃 𝑑img (extracted by frozen DINOv2 [Oquab et al. 2023] encoder) are projected and fused via stack of RefFusionBlocks, each comprising GL-GMHA self-attention over joints, vanilla selfattention, and cross-attention to the image features. The output is set of reference joint queries Qref R𝐽 𝑑 that encode both the skeletal structure and the visual appearance of the reference frame. Temporal pose decoder. Given the reference queries, we predict per-frame joint positions conditioned on the video. For each frame 𝑡, the image features z𝑡 R𝑃 𝑑img are extracted by the same frozen DINOv2 encoder. temporal transformer operates on Qref with perframe image features as conditioning, using GL-GMHA for spatial reasoning across joints and windowed per-joint temporal attention with Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) [Su et al. 2024] across frames. This yields the predicted pose sequence ˆP = { ˆp1, . . . , ˆp𝑇 } R𝑇 𝐽 3. Joints flagged as position-static (i.e., joints whose position relative to the root remains constant throughout the entire groundtruth sequence during data preprocessing) are overwritten with the reference position, ensuring structural consistency. Note that position-static and rotation-static flags are determined independently, since some rigs contain redundant joints whose position is MoCapAnything V2: End-to-End Motion Capture for Arbitrary Skeletons Fig. 3. Framework of MoCapAnything V2. Our method unifies video-to-pose and pose-to-rotation within single end-to-end trainable architecture. The video-to-pose stage consists of reference-conditioned pose prompt encoder (A), which encodes skeleton and image cues into joint prompt, and unified pose decoder (B), which predicts temporally coherent joint positions via cross-attention with video features. The pose-to-rotation stage is formulated as learnable inverse kinematics module, composed of rotation prompt encoder (C) that maps predicted poses into rot prompt, an anchor encoder (D) that encodes reference poserotation pairs to establish consistent rotation coordinate space, and unified rotation decoder (E) that generates animation-ready joint rotations conditioned on the anchor through cross-attention. fixed but whose rotation varies (or vice versa); the rotation-static flag is used analogously in the rotation stage (3.5.3). We regard joint positions as skeleton-shared canonical representation: different skeletons performing the same motion exhibit similar spatial position patterns, making pose transferable intermediate that decouples skeleton-specific coordinate conventions from the motion content (validated in 4.6). Crucially, because the downstream PR is also learnable module (3.5), this pose representation is not frozen by positional-accuracy objective: it is co-adapted during end-to-end training (3.6) to best serve the final rotation objective. This is something factorized pipelines, with their non-differentiable IK handoff, cannot achieve.

sections.title: 3.5 Reference-Conditioned Pose-to-Rotation Module
sections.content: 3.5.1 The Ill-Posedness of Pose-to-Rotation Mapping. Recovering joint rotations from positions alone is fundamentally ill-posed. Rotations are always expressed relative to skeletons rest pose and choice of local coordinate frames. Geometrically, the rest pose fixes the origin of each joints local frame, but not its axes: the same positional trajectory can be expressed by many different rotation sequences under different axis conventions, even before accounting for bone-axis twist (the rotation about bones longitudinal axis), which joint positions do not constrain at all. Conditioning on rest pose alone, which is the static-geometry signal used in prior arbitrary-skeleton work [Gong et al. 2025], therefore supplies only the coordinate origin, and the learning target = 𝑓 (P, o) remains multi-valued; directly learning it leads to unstable convergence and temporally inconsistent predictions. To make PR learnable, we need an explicit axis anchor in addition to the rest-pose origin. In the next subsection we show that single reference poserotation pair from the target asset provides exactly that. 3.5.2 Reference-Conditioned Modeling. We extend the reference signal of prior arbitrary-skeleton work [Gong et al. 2025], which encodes only the target assets static geometry, with single pose rotation pair sampled from the same asset. Intuitively, rest pose supplies the coordinate origin while the reference poserotation pair supplies the coordinate axes: together they fully specify the local frame convention, converting the multi-valued PR mapping into well-constrained conditional prediction task. Concretely, we condition the rotation prediction on three sources of skeleton-specific information: (i) Rest-pose encoding. Rest Pose Encoder takes the bone offsets R𝐽 3 and per-joint semantic embeddings as input, and processes them through stack of GL-GMHA layers to produce rest-pose embedding Erest R𝐽 𝑑 that captures the static skeletal geometry and topology. Gong and Wen, et al. (ii) Reference poserotation pair. single reference frame, comprising known joint position configuration pref and its corresponding ground-truth rotation rref, serves as the coordinate-system anchor. Reference Encoder jointly embeds this pair by projecting the positions and 6D rotations into shared feature space, adding the joint semantic embedding, and processing through GL-GMHA layers conditioned on Erest via FiLM modulation [Perez et al. 2018]. This produces reference feature Cref R𝐽 𝑑 that encodes how specific pose maps to rotations under the target skeletons coordinate conventions. Intuitively, the reference pair tells the model: for this skeletons local coordinate definition, this particular joint configuration corresponds to these rotations. This single anchor suffices to resolve the coordinate-system ambiguity that makes the pose-to-rotation mapping ill-posed. We use single reference pair by default; the effect of additional reference pairs is analyzed in our ablation study (4.5). (iii) Pose encoding. The predicted (or ground-truth) joint position sequence R𝑇 𝐽 3 is encoded by Pose Encoder, which projects positions to the query dimension, adds semantic embeddings, and applies alternating GL-GMHA (for spatial reasoning) and per-joint windowed temporal attention with RoPE (for temporal modeling), optionally modulated by Erest through FiLM. The output is pose feature sequence R𝑇 𝐽 𝑑 . 3.5.3 Rotation Decoder. The rotation decoder predicts per-frame, per-joint 6D rotations from the pose features, conditioned on the reference and rest-pose embeddings. It consists of 𝐿 decoder blocks, each containing: (1) FiLM conditioning on Erest, providing skeleton-specific modulation; (2) Per-joint temporal self-attention (windowed with RoPE) for temporal coherence; (3) GL-GMHA spatial attention (alternating local/global masking) for cross-joint reasoning; (4) Per-joint reference cross-attention, where each joint attends to the reference feature Cref to retrieve rotation anchoring information from the coordinate-system anchor; (5) Feed-forward network with residual connection. The reference cross-attention is applied in the first 𝐿cross 𝐿 decoder layers; the remaining layers rely on the already-integrated reference information. The final layer projects to the 6D rotation output via two-layer MLP head. Joints flagged as rotation-static (determined independently from the position-static flag; see 3.4) are overwritten with the reference rotation, mirroring the static-joint handling in the pose stage. By conditioning on the rest pose, the reference pair, and the input pose jointly, the decoder converts the ill-posed rotation recovery problem into well-constrained conditional prediction task. The model learns statistical motion priors from data, resolving underconstrained degrees of freedom (e.g., twist) that are fundamentally inaccessible to analytical IK.

sections.title: 3.6 End-to-End Training Strategy
sections.content: Joint optimization. With PR cast as learnable neural module (3.5), end-to-end joint training of the entire VPR pipeline becomes possible for the first time in arbitrary-skeleton motion capture. We train the Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation modules jointly: gradients from the rotation loss flow back through the predicted pose and into the visual encoder, so the intermediate pose representation is reshaped not by positional-accuracy objective alone, but by what best serves the final rotation objective. This is the key departure from factorized pipelines, whose non-differentiable IK handoff between VP and PR precludes any such co-adaptation; we quantify the effect in 4.4. Loss function. The total loss combines four terms spanning both the pose and rotation stages: = 𝜆pos Lpos + 𝜆rot Lrot + 𝜆rot_v Lrot_v + 𝜆root Lroot, (2) where Lpos is the per-joint position error between predicted and ground-truth joint positions. For the rotation stage, Lrot measures the geodesic angular error between predicted and ground-truth rotations averaged over all joints, and Lrot_v penalizes the angular velocity difference to promote temporal consistency in the rotation sequence. Lroot additionally re-weights the root joints rotation error, which we found accelerates convergence of the global orientation. All losses are computed with per-joint masking to handle the variable number of joints across different skeletons. We set 𝜆pos=𝜆rot=𝜆rot_v=1.0 and 𝜆root=0.1. Mixed-pose training. distribution gap exists between training, where the Pose-to-Rotation module can receive ground-truth poses, and inference, where it must operate on noisy predicted poses. To bridge this gap, we employ mixed-pose training strategy that stochastically selects, for each sample in batch, whether to feed ground-truth or predicted poses to the rotation module. The probability of using predicted poses follows schedule: 𝑝pred (𝑒) = 𝑝start + (𝑝end 𝑝start) min (cid:16) 1, 𝑒 𝐸warmup (cid:17) , (3) where 𝑒 is the current epoch and 𝐸warmup controls the transition rate. We set 𝑝start=0.1 and 𝑝end=1.0, so that early in training ground-truth poses dominate to ensure stable convergence, and the proportion of predicted poses increases gradually until the model is fully trained on its own pose predictions by the end of the warm-up phase. The choice of 𝐸warmup is studied in 4.4.

sections.title:
4 Experiments
4.1 Dataset and Evaluation Protocol
sections.content: Datasets. We evaluate on two benchmarks spanning diverse skeleton structures: Truebones Zoo [Truebones nd]: 1,038 animal motion sequences (104,715 frames) covering broad range of species and kinematic topologies. The test set (60 sequences) is stratified into Seen (species with abundant training data), Rare (species with limited training data), and Unseen (species never seen during training). Obj: 1,000 samples from Objaverse [Deitke et al. 2023, 2022], containing structurally distinct humanoid and non-animal targets unseen during training, serving as an out-of-distribution stress test. Table 1. Main results on Zoo (Seen/Rare/Unseen) and Obj. Position in cm (); rotation in degrees (). All baselines are trained jointly end-to-end with learnable rotation modules; only V1 uses traditional IK. Best angle error per split in bold. Zoo-Seen Zoo-Rare Zoo-Unseen Obj Method JP HRNet GLoT ViTPose VIBE 32.63 19.66 19.77 19.69 JV 0.53 0.60 0.59 0.54 An 19.86 20.24 20.90 19.67 AV 0.51 0.52 0.52 0.51 JP 34.44 21.60 21.12 20.77 JV 0.59 0.70 0.68 0.63 An 24.72 26.13 25.48 25.06 AV 0.63 0.65 0.63 0.63 JP 37.87 26.13 26.17 26.29 Ours 2.34 0.53 10.73 0.29 2.98 0. 14.38 0.37 3.39 JV 1.45 1.47 1.45 1.45 0. An 24.59 25.95 24.46 25.74 6.54 AV 0.64 0.66 0.63 0.65 0. JP 37.70 22.95 24.16 23.51 JV 1.56 1.70 1.85 1.63 An 31.37 29.07 29.30 28. AV 0.75 0.69 0.70 0.70 3.84 1.05 11.06 0. We additionally evaluate on in-the-wild videos collected from the Internet to assess real-world robustness. Evaluation metrics. We report four metrics spanning spatial accuracy and rotation quality: MPJPE (Mean Per Joint Position Error, cm), MPJVE (Mean Per Joint Velocity Error, cm), Ang. Err (geodesic angle error, ), and AngV Err (angular velocity error, ). To handle large inter-species scale variations, all samples are normalized to [1, 1]3 for training and rescaled to unified 1 m3 cube for evaluation. All metrics are computed with per-joint masking to accommodate varying joint counts. Baselines. We compare against HRNet [Sun et al. 2019], ViTPose [Xu et al. 2022b], VIBE [Kocabas et al. 2020], and GLoT [Shen et al. 2023]. For each baseline, we instantiate both the Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation modules with that methods architecture and train them jointly end-to-end on the same training set with unified skeleton representations. Our most direct point of comparison is MoCapAnything V1 [Gong et al. 2025], which shares the same problem setting and training data, and differs only in that V1 adopts factorized design: learned Video-to-Pose stage with 4D mesh intermediate, followed by constraint-aware analytical IK stage for Pose-to-Rotation, with the two stages optimized independently. Implementation details. We use frozen DINOv2 [Oquab et al. 2023] ViT encoder as the visual backbone. The Video-to-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation modules are trained jointly end-to-end, with the rotation decoder using 𝐿=8 blocks and reference cross-attention in the first 𝐿cross=6 layers. Sequences are processed with length of 𝑇 =48 frames and per-joint temporal attention uses window size of 5. The model is trained with Adam on 8 V100 for 60 epochs with batch size of 8, taking roughly one day. Loss weights are set to 𝜆pos=𝜆rot=𝜆rot_v=1.0 and 𝜆root=0.1. The mixed-pose schedule uses 𝑝start=0.1, 𝑝end=1.0, and 𝐸warmup=30. The maximum joint count is set to 150 to accommodate the largest skeleton in our training data (143 joints); this limit is straightforward to adjust for other datasets.

sections.title: 4.2 Comparison with Baselines
sections.content: Table 1 presents comprehensive comparison across all four evaluation splits. Our method achieves consistently strong performance across all splits. On positional metrics, we outperform V1 under the realistic predicted-mesh configuration  (Table 2)  . The largest improvements are on rotation metrics. Although the learned baselines also adopt an end-to-end VPR pipeline with learnable rotation modules, MoCapAnything V2: End-to-End Motion Capture for Arbitrary Skeletons Table 2. V1 vs. Ours under different mesh configurations. GT Mesh = ground-truth mesh; Pred Mesh = predicted mesh; Ours removes mesh entirely. For fair comparison with V1 (which was trained on Zoo only), all models in this table are both trained and evaluated on Zoo only; the Ours numbers therefore differ slightly from those in Table 1, where the model is trained on Zoo+Obj. Best angle error per split in bold. Zoo-Seen Zoo-Rare Zoo-Unseen Config JP JV An V1 (GT Mesh+IK) V1 (Pred Mesh+IK) 1.06 3.30 0.44 0.67 17.47 20.02 AV 2.18 2. JP JV An 1.28 4.19 0.37 0.55 18.52 19. AV 2.08 2.18 JP JV An 1.76 4. 0.36 0.93 20.56 22.04 Ours 2.20 0.53 10. 0.30 2.86 0.59 14.36 0.37 3. 0.98 6.68 AV 3.10 3.37 0.18 they lack the reference poserotation conditioning and topologyaware attention needed to resolve coordinate-axis ambiguity on arbitrary skeletons, while V1 instead recovers rotations through analytical IK. Both regimes cap rotation quality at roughly 20 average angle error and frequently produce visual artifacts such as joint spinning. Our reference-conditioned design, combined with end-to-end VPR training, reduces the average angle error to approximately 10 and substantially lowers the angular velocity error, indicating more accurate and temporally stable rotation predictions. The improvement is most pronounced on Zoo-Unseen, where the angle error (6.54) is actually lower than on Zoo-Seen (10.73) and Zoo-Rare (14.38); inspection of the test sequences reveals that the Unseen split predominantly contains common, locomotion-style motions (e.g., running, walking) that lie well within the motion distribution seen during training, making rotation recovery easier once the coordinate axes are anchored by the reference poserotation pair.

sections.title: 4.3 End-to-End vs. Factorized Design: Comparison with V1
sections.content: V1 [Gong et al. 2025] differs from our method along two simultaneous axes: (i) presence of 4D mesh intermediate between video and pose, and (ii) factorized learned-VP + analytical-IK PR design versus our end-to-end learnable VPR. We compare against V1 under different mesh configurations in Table 2 to disentangle the two effects. Three observations emerge. First, V1 with ground-truth mesh achieves the lowest positional error, confirming that geometric information is beneficial when it is accurate. However, this setting is not realistic since ground-truth mesh is unavailable at inference time. Second, V1 with predicted mesh performs markedly worse than our mesh-free model, showing that the mesh intermediate becomes liability once prediction noise enters the pipeline: the mesh bridge in V1s factorized design amplifies rather than mitigates upstream error. Third, our end-to-end learnable VPR achieves competitive pose accuracy compared to V1 (GT Mesh) without using any mesh, while substantially outperforming all V1 variants on rotation metrics. This validates two claims simultaneously: the mesh intermediate is not essential, and making PR learnable and jointly trained with VP yields clear rotation-quality advantage over V1s learned VP + analytical IK pipeline. Focusing on rotation quality, V1s constraint-aware IK produces angle errors in the range of 1720 and frequently exhibits visual artifacts such as joint spinning and limb flipping, particularly on Gong and Wen, et al. Table 3. Ablation of training strategies on Zoo (Seen/Rare/Unseen) and Obj. Position in cm; rotation in degrees (). Best angle error per split in bold. Table 4. Our approach reduces the average angle error to approximately 10 and substantially lowers the angular velocity error, leading to more accurate and temporally stable rotations. Two factors drive this improvement, as further analyzed in subsequent sections: (a) end-to-end joint training of VPR allows the intermediate pose representation to adapt to the rotation objective rather than being constrained by purely positional one (4.4), and (b) the reference poserotation pair makes learnable PR feasible by anchoring the local coordinate system and resolving rotational ambiguity (4.5). While stronger mesh predictor could potentially narrow the positional gap to V1 (GT Mesh), it would incur significant computational overhead (4.10); in contrast, our mesh-free pipeline achieves favorable balance between accuracy, robustness, and inference efficiency.

sections.title: Claim
sections.content: This experiment directly tests the central claim of our framework: making PR learnable is not sufficient on its own; its benefit arises from enabling end-to-end gradient coupling with VP. We therefore compare four training regimes: gradient-detached variant (where gradients from PR do not flow back to VP), end-to-end training with ground-truth poses only, end-to-end training with predicted poses only, and our default mixed-pose end-to-end training. Results are shown in Table 3. The results yield three findings. First, enabling gradient flow is critical. Compared to the gradient-detached variant, joint training reduces the Zoo-Unseen angle error from 7.82 to 6.54, showing that the benefit of learnable PR module comes from end-toend adaptation rather than standalone modeling. Second, GT-only training suffers from distribution gap and fails on Zoo-Unseen (13.28 vs. 6.54), while Pred-only training is unstable due to noisy early predictions. Finally, the mixed-pose strategy achieves the best balance. Varying the warm-up length (𝐸𝑤) shows consistent performance across 𝐸𝑤 [20, 50], indicating that the method is robust to the mixing schedule. We use 𝐸𝑤=30 by default.

sections.title: 4.5 Pose-to-Rotation Module Analysis
sections.content: Reference pair and rest pose. Table 4 examines the contributions of the reference poserotation pair and the rest-pose encoding. This ablation empirically confirms the theoretical analysis of 3.5.1, namely the origin/axes decomposition of the reference signal. Zoo-Seen Zoo-Rare Zoo-Unseen Obj Architecture JP JV An Direct (VR) Latent + Aux Full (explicit pose, Ours) 2.07 2.34 0.47 0.53 9.32 9.06 10.73 AV 0.26 0.25 0.29 JP JV An 2.40 2.98 0.53 0.61 12.71 11.85 14.38 AV 0.34 0.31 0.37 JP JV An 2.88 3.39 0.89 0. 23.73 23.57 6.54 AV 0.62 0.62 0.17 JP JV An 3.82 3.84 1.04 1.05 11.16 10.71 11.06 AV 0.28 0.27 0.30 its coordinate convention with the training distribution, all four configurations reach comparable angle errors in the 1015 range: the axis convention is effectively memorized from training data, so the explicit axis anchor provided by the reference pair offers little additional gain. The picture changes dramatically on Zoo-Unseen, whose axis conventions the model has not seen before. Without the reference pair, the angle error jumps to 24.0524.26: rest pose alone supplies the coordinate origin but not enough information about the axes, leaving the PR hard to learn. Adding the reference pair, which supplies the missing axes, drops the error to 7.37, and combining both reference pair and rest pose yields the best result of 6.54. The rest pose thus provides smaller but consistent additional benefit on top of the reference, acting as complementary structural context.

sections.title: 4.6 Role of Pose as Intermediate Representation
sections.content: To validate the necessity of explicit joint positions as an intermediate representation, we compare three architectural variants in Table 5. Three observations emerge. First, the Direct (VR) variant, which predicts rotations directly from video, achieves comparable angle error on Zoo-Seen and Zoo-Rare (9.32 / 12.71), but degrades significantly on Zoo-Unseen (23.73). This indicates that without an explicit pose intermediate, the model struggles to generalize across unseen skeletons. Second, the Latent + Aux variant, which introduces latent representation supervised by pose loss, achieves strong performance on Zoo-Seen and Zoo-Rare (9.06 / 11.85), but similarly fails on Zoo-Unseen (23.57). This suggests that an implicit pose representation, even when supervised, does not transfer well across different skeleton topologies. Third, our full model, which uses explicit joint positions as an intermediate, significantly improves rotation generalization on unseen skeletons (6.54), while remaining competitive on seen and rare splits. These results suggest that joint positions provide skeleton-shared spatial representation, whereas rotations are inherently skeleton-dependent. The explicit pose intermediate thus acts as structural bottleneck that separates Table 6. Ablation of attention mechanisms. Position in cm; rotation in degrees (). All variants use the same backbone; only the spatial attention pattern differs. GMHA (all-global) is the original uniform formulation from [Gat et al. 2025] in which every layer attends over all joints; All-local applies the ancestor mask at every layer; GL-GMHA (Ours) alternates between local and global layers. sections.title: 4.7 GL-GMHA Attention Analysis
sections.content: Table 6 evaluates the effectiveness of the Global-Local extension to graph-guided attention. The ablation confirms the value of the alternating design. Removing graph biases entirely (Full Attn) yields the worst results on most metrics, as the model loses the structural inductive bias that encodes joint relationships. The original uniform GMHA [Gat et al. 2025], which attends globally in every layer, is strong baseline but does not explicitly model kinematic-chain locality. The Alllocal variant, which restricts attention to ancestor paths in every layer, can capture intra-branch dependencies but loses cross-branch coordination, with its angle error on Zoo-Rare jumping to 16.91 and on Zoo-Unseen to 10.37, well above Ours. Our alternating GL-GMHA achieves consistently lower angle error than uniform GMHA across all four splits, by interleaving the two attention patterns at every pair of layers, validating that kinematic-chain locality and skeleton-global coordination are complementary rather than redundant.

sections.title: 4.10 Efficiency Analysis
sections.content: Our efficiency gain comes from eliminating both the mesh prediction stage and the analytical IK solver. For 120-frame input sequence, V1 requires approximately 40 for feature extraction, 15 min for mesh reconstruction, 20 for pose estimation, and an additional 5 min for IK optimization, resulting in total runtime of over 20 minutes. In contrast, our method retains the same feature extraction cost (40 s), but directly predicts pose and rotation in single forward pass within 10 s, reducing the overall runtime to under 1 minute. This corresponds to 20 speedup in practice. The improvement stems from two design choices: removing the mesh intermediate eliminates heavy and error-prone reconstruction stage, while replacing analytical IK with learned rotation decoder avoids iterative per-frame optimization and enables efficient batched computation. Importantly, this efficiency gain does not come at the cost of accuracy. Averaged over Zoo-Seen/Rare/Unseen  (Table 2)  , our method achieves an angle error of 10.6, compared to 18.9 for V1 with ground-truth mesh and 20.1 with predicted mesh, demonstrating favorable trade-off between speed and accuracy.

sections.title: 4.11 Qualitative Results
sections.content: We study the effect of model depth by jointly scaling both the Videoto-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation modules  (Table 7)  . Increasing the depth from 6 to 8 layers improves rotation accuracy, while further increasing to 12 layers degrades performance across all splits. All figures above are animated GIFs (Click and play); we recommend opening the PDF in Adobe Reader to view the animations, or viewing the corresponding videos on our project homepage for the best experience. Gong and Wen, et al. Fig. 5. MoCap demo across domains. Row 1: Objaverse assets; Row 2: Truebones Zoo; Rows 34: in-the-wild videos. Results are shown from multiple viewpoints, demonstrating accurate mocap on arbitrary subjects. Fig. 6. Dance demo. Given single input video (center), our method performs mocap on humanoid skeleton (left) and retargets the motion to an animal skeleton (right). Rotation quality: V1 vs. V2. Figure 4 compares our learning-based rotation recovery (V2) against the traditional IK-based optimization used in V1. V1 frequently exhibits joint spinning and limb flipping, particularly on joints with large twist components, since per-frame IK lacks temporal context and reference priors. In contrast, V2 produces natural, temporally continuous rotations, confirming the effectiveness of reference-conditioned rotation modeling. MoCap across Objaverse, Zoo, and in-the-wild. Figure 5 shows mocap results across three domains: Objaverse assets (row 1), Truebones Zoo (row 2), and in-the-wild Internet videos (rows 34), rendered from multiple viewpoints. Despite large variations in appearance, shape, and capture conditions, our method produces accurate and temporally consistent 3D motion, demonstrating that it generalizes to arbitrary subjects rather than being tied to specific training distribution. Unified mocap and cross-skeleton retargeting. distinctive feature of our framework is that single input video can simultaneously drive mocap on one skeleton and retargeting to another, without any skeleton-specific training. Figures 6 illustrate this on two dance clips: given the input in the center, the left column shows mocap on humanoid skeleton, while the right column shows retargeting to an animal skeleton. The retargeted motion preserves the rhythm and semantics of the source while respecting the target skeletons topology, enabling flexible cross-species motion transfer from single video.

sections.title: 5 Limitations
sections.content: Our method has three main limitations. First, the Pose-to-Rotation decoder relies on motion priors learned from the training distribution; for motions that lie well outside this distribution (for example, forcing quadruped skeleton into bipedal arms-raised pose it would never perform naturally), the decoder may produce implausible rotations, even when the upstream pose prediction remains reasonable. Expanding the diversity of (skeleton, motion) combinations in the training data is the most direct path to addressing this. Second, the framework assumes single foreground subject with minimal camera motion and does not explicitly handle heavy occlusion, since such cases are under-represented in our training data. Incorporating occlusion-aware data augmentation or segmentation-conditioned visual backbone is promising direction. Third, per-species coverage in the training set is uneven (Truebones Zoo contains roughly 1,000 sequences across only few dozen species), so rotation quality on rarely seen species is bounded by data scarcity rather than by the modeling framework itself.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We have presented the first fully end-to-end framework for arbitraryskeleton motion capture from monocular video, in which both Videoto-Pose and Pose-to-Rotation are learnable neural modules and are jointly trained. The enabler is simple extension of the reference signal used by prior work: adding single poserotation pair from the target asset, which anchors the coordinate axes of each joints local frame and thereby converts the ill-posed PR mapping into well-constrained conditional prediction task. Once PR is learnable, joint training lets the intermediate pose representation reshape itself to serve the final rotation objective. This is something factorized pipelines, with their non-differentiable IK handoff, cannot achieve. On Truebones Zoo and Objaverse, our approach reduces the average rotation angle error from 17 (V1s factorized learned-VP + analytical-IK pipeline) to 10, with the largest improvements on unseen skeletons whose axis conventions rest-pose structure alone cannot disambiguate. Removing the mesh intermediate used by prior work as video-to-joint bridge further yields 20 faster inference, since predicted-mesh errors compound through the pipeline rather than providing useful information. We hope that this shift, from factorized learn-then-solve pipelines to unified end-to-end learnable VPR, provides useful foundation for future work in universal character animation and crossskeleton motion understanding.
